content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • •• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 23, 1976

A-76-291

Lyman Peterson
Acting Superintendent
River Delta Unified School District
445 Montezuma
Rio Vista, California 94571

Dear Mr. Peterson:

Governor Brown has provided our agency with a copy of the
resolution adopted by the board of your school district on
August 3, 1976. Essentially, the resolution asks the Legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to exempt
elected school board members from the financial disclosure
provisions of Government Code Sections 87100, et seq.

It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain
of their private financial interests. However, under existing
law, school districts are governmental agencies; members of
boards of education as well as certain administrative personnel
within the school districts "make or participate in the making
of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect private financial
interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires that
school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable to
members and officials who are capable of affecting such financial
interests. Only those financial interests which could be affected
by the filer's official actions must be disclosed, and it is
the school district which has the first and primary responsibility
to identify these potential conflicts of interest and provide
for their disclosure. Hopefully, individuals who object to
overly broad disclosure will take an active role in the design
of each code's disclosure requirements, so that intrusion of
personal privacy will be kept at a minimum consistent with the
objective of preventing or disclosing conflicts of interest.

The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to and
has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies
in drafting codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests which
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.

Mr. Lyman Peterson
September 23, 1976
Page Two


The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an
initiative. Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:

     Assets and income of public officials which
     may be materially affected by their official
     actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
     circumstances the officials should be dis-
     qualified from acting in order that conflicts
     of interest may be avoided.

                                    Gov. Code § 81002(g).

Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the requirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.

     Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the right of school
board members to express their views, I believe the proposal
is without merit. School boards make decisions of great con-
sequence to their communities. Like other public officials,
school board members serve in a position of public trust; and
the people have the right to be assured that their decisions
will be made free from bias caused by their own financial
interests. Disclosure of any financial interests which could
foreseeably be affected by their official actions is a reason-
able and effective means of preventing conflicts of interest.
In recent years the California Supreme Court and the courts of
many other states have ruled that such disclosure requirements
are valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of
the official is justified by the important public interest
that is served.

     I hope that this information is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at (916) 322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:plh

RESOLUTION #117

CONFLICT OF INTEREST

WHEREAS, public school boards are required by public law (Government Code
Sections 87100 et seq.) to prepare and adopt a district Conflict of Interest
Code, and

WHEREAS, board members of said school boards are required by law to file a
disclosure statement disclosing their interest and those of their immediate
families in investments, real property, and income, and

WHEREAS, these disclosure statements are by law made available for public
scrutiny, and

WHEREAS, other sections of the Government Code (Section 1090 et seq.) and
Title 2, California Administrative Code (Section 18700 et seq.) and the Education Code (Section 1174) provide the needed legal control of board member
actions, and

WHEREAS, the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" is an infringement on
the rights of private citizens and their immediate families and is an invasion
of personal privacy of board members and their immediate families, and

WHEREAS, this act can require personal expenses for attorney and accountant
fees by board members holding non-paying elective board positions, and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of this Code will encourage private
investigative snooping by persons bent on personal vendettas, and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of the California "Political Reform
Act of 1974" by boards will result in the resignation of untold numbers of board
members throughout the state, and

WHEREAS, school districts and communities of the State need citizens with financial
solvency, business acumen, and personal stability to serve as board members.

IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED by the Board of Education of the River Delta Unified School
District to publicly state its opposition to the inclusion of elected public school board
members within the requirements of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" and
it is resolved to urge its legislators to take the necessary action to bring about the
changes needed to exclude school board members from the requirements of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974".

AYES:    Hamilton, Lawrence, Lewallen, Merwin, Silva, Upham

NOES:    None

ABSENT: Himoto

I hereby certify that the foregoing is a true and correct transcript of a resolution duly
adopted and affirmed by a formal vote of the members of the Governing Board of the
River Delta Unified School District at a duly constituted regular meeting of the Board
which was open to the public on August 3, 1978, as it appears upon the minutes of the
said meeting of the said Governing Board. [illegible signature and date]

sections.question_synthetic: This document is not a legal question but rather a formal resolution by the River Delta Unified School District Board of Education opposing the application of the California Political Reform Act of 1974 to elected school board members.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a formal resolution, not an FPPC advice letter or incoming request. Contains no QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections. The document expresses opposition to FPPC requirements rather than seeking guidance. Confidence slightly reduced due to image quality and partial illegibility of signature/attestation section.

embedding.summary: This is Resolution #117 adopted by the River Delta Unified School District Board of Education on August 3, 1976, formally opposing the inclusion of elected public school board members in the requirements of the California Political Reform Act of 1974 and urging legislators to exclude school boards from the Act's disclosure and conflict of interest requirements.
